metadata.title: Quick-fire: When silence is the lie
metadata.author: patterns-of-choice editorial draft
metadata.design_intent: Sixth truth-telling quick-fire. Prior rounds covered active small lies (qf-001), audience-variation (qf-003), past misrepresentations resurfacing (qf-005), honest-feedback gradient (qf-007), and honesty about uncertainty (qf-009). This round centers the omission side: situations where the user is NOT being directly asked but the absence of speech is itself a stance. Tests whether users who reliably avoid commission lies also avoid omission lies, or whether there's a characteristic gap (omission tolerated where commission isn't) that the instrument should be able to detect.
metadata.domain_anchor: Gentilin 2016 The Origins of Ethical Failures on the silence-of-bystanders pattern; behavioral-ethics literature on bounded ethicality (Bazerman & Tenbrunsel 2011) where omissions are characteristically rated as less wrong than commissions even when consequences are equivalent; Spranca/Minsk/Baron 1991 'Omission and commission in judgment and choice' as the foundational asymmetry result.

items.prompt: In a group conversation, someone confidently misstates a fact you happen to know clearly. The misstatement is not high-stakes but is wrong. No one else corrects it.

options.text: Correct it briefly and plainly. 'Actually I think it's X — happy to be wrong if you have a source.'
Let it go. It's not material, and correcting unprompted shifts the room's tone in a way that costs more than the inaccuracy.

items.prompt: A new colleague repeats an inaccurate story about an absent person you know well. The story would shape how the colleague treats this person going forward.

options.text: Correct it directly: 'I know that situation differently — here's what actually happened.'
Stay out of it. Defending the absent person uninvited can read as taking sides; let it play out and address it if it becomes consequential.

items.prompt: You overhear two colleagues making a plan based on a premise you know to be wrong. They didn't include you in the conversation; the wrong premise would lead them in a costly direction.

options.text: Step in: 'Sorry to interrupt — I overheard, and I think the premise is off. Worth checking before you commit.'
Let them proceed. They didn't ask you in; intervening on overheard material crosses a small but real boundary.

items.prompt: A close friend asks 'is everything OK between us?' You've been holding a real concern about the friendship for weeks — nothing dramatic, but real. They are explicitly opening the door.

options.text: Name it. 'Actually — yes, there's something I've been sitting with. Can we talk about it?'
Deflect mildly: 'Yeah, we're good — anything specific you were noticing?' Buys time to think about whether the concern survives the friendship.

items.prompt: A meeting decision is being finalized based on information you know is incomplete. You're junior to the room; speaking up has some social cost. The decision isn't catastrophic if wrong but isn't trivial either.

options.text: Speak up: 'Before we lock this — I want to flag a piece I think is missing.' Frame it as information, not objection.
Stay quiet. You can raise the gap afterward with whoever sponsored the call; not every flag has to land in real time.

items.prompt: You're being praised in a meeting for something. The credit is overstated — you did some of the work, but a substantial portion was someone else's. They aren't in the room.

options.text: Redirect the credit in the moment: 'Thanks — though [name] did the heavier piece on this and I want to make sure that's on the record.'
Accept the praise; mention the colleague later. Correcting in the moment can derail the room's energy; the truth is better placed in a follow-up.
